title: Polymer clay bead set
subtitle: A set of round, oval, and tube beads baked in a home oven
excerpt: Polymer clay beads are shaped by hand, pierced before baking, and hardened in a home oven at 130°C for 30 minutes. No kiln is needed: the oven does the curing. The result is a set of lightweight, durable beads in any colour that can be strung into a necklace or bracelet. Polymer clay is sold in small blocks in many colours.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay bead technique. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mouldable PVC-based modelling material that hardens permanently when baked in a home oven at 110–135°C. Sold in small blocks in many colours.
The process of hardening polymer clay by baking it in an oven. Uncured polymer clay is soft and flexible; cured clay is hard and durable.

content.text: beads are the fastest introduction to the material: the shapes are simple, the

step is straightforward, and the results are usable the same day. A set of beads in one or two colours makes a necklace or bracelet. Mix colours by kneading two portions of clay together before shaping.

attrs.title: Oven temperature
attrs.body: Verify the actual oven temperature with a thermometer before baking; polymer clay burns above 175°C.

items.name: Polymer clay, one or two colours
Baking tray (dedicated to clay, not food)

content.text: Knead the clay between your palms for two to three minutes until it is soft and pliable without cracks when bent. Cold clay from the packet is stiff and crumbles when shaped without conditioning. Warm hands speed up conditioning; if the clay is very stiff, hold the block in your palms for a minute before kneading.

Pinch off a piece of clay about the size of a large pea. Roll it between both palms in a circular motion for a smooth sphere. For an oval bead, roll the sphere once or twice more with the palms slightly apart. For a tube bead, roll back and forth on a smooth surface with one finger to elongate. Aim for consistent sizes: cut equal-weight pieces from the conditioned clay block before rolling.

Hold the bead lightly and push the needle tool straight through the centre in one firm movement. Withdraw the needle, turn the bead 180 degrees, and pierce from the other side to meet the first hole. This two-direction method prevents a funnel-shaped hole. The hole should be 0.5 to 1 mm wider than the stringing cord. Widen by rotating the needle tool gently once it is through.

Preheat the oven to 130°C, confirmed with the oven thermometer. Thread the beads onto a length of knitting needle or skewer so they do not flat-spot on the tray. Lay the skewer across the baking tray, which raises the beads off the surface. Bake for 30 minutes. Remove and leave to cool completely before handling, hot polymer clay is soft and deforms easily.
Roll two colours together incompletely for a marble effect before shaping the bead.
Press a stamp or the tip of a knitting needle lightly into the bead surface before baking for textured decoration.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when making polymer clay beads.

items.symptom: Beads are soft and flexible after baking
items.cause: Oven temperature was too low, or bake time too short.
items.fix: Check the oven temperature with a thermometer, dial settings are unreliable. Return beads to the oven for a further 15 minutes at the correct temperature. Properly cured polymer clay is hard and does not flex when cool.

items.symptom: Beads have flat spots on one side
items.cause: Beads rested on the tray surface during baking.
items.fix: Thread beads onto a skewer or length of wire before baking so they hang free of the tray surface.
